cataloging_subject.label: Ave Maria
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The complete multilingual comparative article selected, transcribed, edited, and published by Wilhelm Heuser in 1904 and represented as CME00097: German commentary plus five distinct anonymous medieval verse items from three manuscripts. It is not a single medieval Ave Maria, any complete manuscript witness, the U-M digital encoding, the repository XML, or a facsimile.

catalog_summary.title: Ave Maria
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous medieval contributors
catalog_summary.date: Texts attested in fourteenth- and first-half-fifteenth-century English manuscripts; edited selection published 1904
catalog_summary.region: Medieval texts transmitted in English manuscript witnesses; article compiled in Cambridge and published in Halle an der Saale

notes: This is Heuser's publication title for the comparative selection, not a manuscript-supplied collective title for all five medieval texts.

titles.value: Ave Maria: Five Medieval Texts Edited by Wilhelm Heuser

assertion.notes: A project-supplied clarification for discovery, not a published alternate title.

agents.name: Wilhelm Heuser
notes: The stable authority record identifies the Anglist and Oberlehrer; a reliable death date was not established in this review.

assertion.notes: No reviewed source securely attributes any of the five medieval items to a named historical author.

notes: The collective display summarizes multiple anonymous creators or transmitters; it does not assert common authorship.

scope.description: Selection and comparative organization of five items from three manuscript witnesses
German introductions, comparative argument, manuscript notes, and signed closing

date_statements.display: Compiled by Wilhelm Heuser no later than 1904
notes: This dates the modern edited selection, not the composition of its medieval texts.

date_statements.display: Attested in Bodleian MS Douce 126, produced in the first half of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: DIMEV records only this witness for record 1728; the date is manuscript production, not a proved composition date.

date_statements.display: Attested in CUL MS Gg.1.1, produced in the first half of the fourteenth century after 1307

assertion.notes: This is the represented witness's production range; the hymn's composition date and any earlier witnesses remain unknown.

date_statements.display: The represented Gg.4.32 copies are in a manuscript dated broadly to the fourteenth century; their composition dates and relative priority are unknown

assertion.notes: The type remains unknown because the reviewed evidence does not prove that Gg.4.32 is the first surviving attestation of the four-line salutation, which DIMEV also records in other witnesses.
The signed article closes with a Cambridge dateline; this supports the place of Heuser's editorial work, not the medieval works' composition.

scope.description: Transmission in manuscript witnesses produced or held in England

assertion.notes: This association concerns the manuscript tradition and does not assign England as the place of composition for every text.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: A separate Middle English line quoted in the Section 2 German headnote is editorial paratext, not a sixth medieval item.

language_statements.label: Anglo-Norman French
notes: The repository XML's root language declaration omits this language even though the complete Section 2 text is Anglo-Norman.

form_statements.description: The complete edited selection intentionally combines substantial German prose commentary with five verse texts.
German introductions and comparative commentary, with brief quoted verse and footnotes.
All five medieval items are versified; the Section 4 Latin prose salutation serves as an epigraph to its twelve quatrains.

assertion.notes: DIMEV explicitly classifies the Middle English items as hymns to the Virgin; the Anglo-Norman and Latin items are parallel versified Ave Maria texts.

summaries.value: Wilhelm Heuser's 1904 article brings together five anonymous medieval expansions of the Ave Maria from three manuscripts: a seventeen-quatrain Middle English hymn from Bodleian MS Douce 126; a sixteen-quatrain Anglo-Norman hymn from CUL MS Gg.1.1; a four-line Middle English salutation and a fifteen-stanza Middle English hymn from CUL MS Gg.4.32; and a twelve-quatrain Latin hymn that immediately precedes the English material in Gg.4.32. German introductions compare their structure, rhyme, language, and possible relationships, while local notes report manuscript readings and expansions.

Section 1: ‘Hayle mayden of maydyns thorȝht worde consaywyng’ (68 lines). Section 2: ‘Ave tresduce Marie’ (64 lines), preceded by German comparison and one quoted Middle English line from another poem. Section 3: ‘Heil Marie, ful of grace’ (4 lines) followed by ‘Heil and holi ay be þi name’ (120 lines). Section 4: ‘Ave, dei genitrix, semper deo grata’ (48 lines).

extent.description: At the modeled encoded-unit boundary, 67 counted items comprise six editorial prose paragraphs and 61 verse groups, organized as one noncontiguous editorial-frame part and five distinct medieval works. The whole item contains 305 verse-line elements: 304 core lines in the five works (17/68, 16/64, 1/4, 15/120, and 12/48) plus one Middle English comparison line quoted inside the German Section 2 headnote. The XML has only 60 LG because the four-line salutation is an EPIGRAPH/Q rather than LG. The editorial quotation is reported separately in the frame metrics and must not inflate any direct medieval work's line count.

content_parts.label: Heuser's editorial frame
content_parts.summary: German introductions and comparative discussion for all four sections, eight manuscript/editorial notes, a one-line Middle English comparison quotation in the Section 2 headnote, manuscript and printed-page milestones, and the signed Cambridge closing.

locators.value: All DIV2/HEADNOTE and NOTE elements; DIV1 closing
320–330, interleaved with the five edited texts

notes: The embedded Middle English line ‘Be myn help and my fultum...’ is a quotation from a different Anglo-Norman poem discussed by Heuser, not a sixth cataloged item.

content_parts.label: Hail Maiden of Maidens through Word Conceiving
content_parts.summary: An anonymous Middle English hymn based on the Angelic Salutation, beginning ‘Hayle mayden of maydyns thorȝht worde consaywyng’; seventeen long quatrains address Mary through the successive words of the Ave Maria.
notes: DIMEV record 1728 / IMEV-NIMEV 1059.
DIMEV's aabb rhyme classification conflicts with Heuser's continuous-rhyme description and remains unresolved pending manuscript-image review.

content_parts.label: Ave tresduce Marie
content_parts.summary: An anonymous Anglo-Norman hymn of sixteen monorhyme quatrains, each stanza beginning with a successive word of the Angelic Salutation. Heuser prints the complete text after noting that Paul Meyer had previously printed only its first two lines.
notes: The final two poem lines occupy the top of fol. 265r; the same leaf continues with the next manuscript text.

content_parts.label: Hail Mary Full of Grace / God Is with Thee in Every Place
content_parts.summary: A four-line anonymous Middle English Ave Maria that serves as the heading or acrostic key to the following fifteen-stanza hymn.
notes: DIMEV record 1731 / IMEV-NIMEV 1062 also records variant copies in Cambridge, Emmanuel College MS 27 and BL Cotton Cleopatra B VI; Heuser's selected text is the Gg.4.32 copy.
No synthetic crosswalk is asserted between Heuser's and DIMEV's folio references.

content_parts.label: Hail and Holy Ay Be Thy Name
content_parts.summary: An anonymous Middle English hymn to the Virgin in fifteen eight-line stanzas. Each stanza begins with a successive word from the preceding four-line salutation, producing a type of acrostic based on the Angelic Salutation.

locators.value: Heuser working sequence: fols. 14v–15v; DIMEV: ff. 23vb–24va

notes: DIMEV record 1680 / IMEV-NIMEV 1024; rhyme pattern abababab.
The foliation systems remain unreconciled without current manuscript images or repository guidance.

content_parts.label: Ave, Dei Genitrix, Semper Deo Grata
content_parts.summary: An anonymous Latin Ave Maria in twelve quatrains. Heuser observes that its final line in each stanza shifts from the hymn's trochaic rhythm toward hexameter and that it immediately precedes the English material in Gg.4.32.

locators.value: Heuser: fol. 14v; M. R. James: legacy fol. 14v / bracketed current fol. 22v at opening

notes: The reviewed sources secure the opening and adjacency but do not supply a verified current end folio.

related_works.title: Hail Maiden of Maidens through Word Conceiving
Hail Mary Full of Grace / God Is with Thee in Every Place
Hail and Holy Ay Be Thy Name
Ave, Dei Genitrix, Semper Deo Grata

evidence.citation: CME00097 XML source in the pinned CME submodule.
evidence.summary: The XML identifies CME00097, supplies the preferred title, and contains the complete four-section edited selection.

evidence.citation: CME00097 section headnotes and manuscript labels.
evidence.summary: Heuser introduces the texts, names all three manuscript witnesses, distinguishes related texts from sources, discusses verse structure and language, and supplies the working folio references used in the edition.
The article closes with the dateline Cambridge and signature W. Heuser, supporting editorial responsibility and place of compilation.

evidence.citation: Structural and content count of CME00097.
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1; DIV2, HEAD, P, LG, L, EPIGRAPH, NOTE, PB, and MILESTONE descendants
evidence.summary: The XML contains four DIV2 sections, 60 LG elements, 305 L elements, six P elements, eight NOTE elements, eleven PB elements, and seven MILESTONE elements. Content inspection separates five medieval items totaling 61 intellectual verse groups and 304 core lines from one four-line epigraph encoded outside LG and one paratext quotation encoded as L.
notes: Item metrics are 17/68, 16/64, 1/4, 15/120, and 12/48 verse groups/lines.
The raw 305 L count is not the five-item line extent because it includes the separate Middle English quotation in the German Section 2 headnote.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Ave Maria / [ed. W. Heuser], CME00097.
evidence.summary: Supports title, editor display, 1904 publication data, stable identifier, public full text, and the U-M rights statement.
Primary edition evidence for the cataloging boundary, title, responsibility, section structure, languages, forms, contents, source manuscript statements, and Cambridge closing.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, records 1728, 1731, and 1680.
evidence.summary: Supports the three Middle English item identities, anonymous status, hymn/Marian subjects, verse extents, IMEV/NIMEV identifiers, witness loci, and Heuser edition citations.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian and Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries record for MS Douce 126.
evidence.summary: Supports the current shelfmark, first-half fifteenth-century date, England origin, parchment support, and the lack of target coverage in the one-image public object.

evidence.citation: Cambridge Digital Library MS Gg.1.1 manifest and visually inspected target canvases.
evidence.summary: Supports the manuscript date, England origin, Anglo-Norman language and direct visual mapping of the complete Section 2 hymn to fols. 264r–265r.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, unpublished description of CUL MS Gg.4.32, Cambridge repository transcription.
evidence.summary: Supports Gg.4.32's material, extent, dating, languages, contents, the Latin-before-English sequence, and dual foliation evidence.

evidence.citation: Biblissima+ record for Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.32.
evidence.summary: Supports current identity, holding repository, and the broad fourteenth-century Jonas dating; the generated empty IIIF Collection is separately recorded in lineage.

open_questions.question: When and where were the five anonymous medieval texts composed, as distinct from the production dates and locations of their represented witnesses?
next_steps: Review later critical editions and language studies for each independently identified text.
Keep any dialect localization separate from place of composition and manuscript production.
notes: The current date and region summary must remain witness- and edition-qualified rather than presenting manuscript production as composition.

open_questions.question: How do Heuser's working foliation, M. R. James's legacy/bracketed references, and DIMEV's loci for the Gg.4.32 items map to the current manuscript leaves?
next_steps: Ask Cambridge University Library for a foliation concordance or complete reproduction.
Align incipits, explicits, columns, and folio labels directly if images become available.
notes: Exact current folio and canvas ranges for three of the five items cannot be asserted until a repository crosswalk or complete facsimile is available.

open_questions.question: Does direct inspection of Douce 126 fols. 92r–92v support Heuser's continuous-rhyme description or DIMEV's aabb classification for record 1728?
next_steps: Request or locate images of fols. 92r–92v.
Consult Saupe's 1998 edition and DIMEV revision history.
notes: No rhyme scheme is exposed as normalized metadata until the conflict is resolved.

The article's medieval item count is five, not four: the four-line Middle English salutation in Section 3 and the following fifteen-stanza hymn are separately indexed works even though Heuser presents them together.
The raw XML L count is 305, while the five core medieval items total 304 verse lines; the extra line is a Middle English quotation embedded in Heuser's German Section 2 headnote.
The preferred title and whole-work mixed form describe the cataloged edited selection. Each five medieval content parts remains verse-only and anonymous.
Anglo-Norman code xno is added from direct evidence even though the source XML's TEXT/@LANG declares only Middle English, German, and Latin.
